title: Geometry and arithmetic of sphere packings
speaker: Alex Kontorovich
abstract: We introduce the notion of a "crystallographic sphere packing," which generalizes the classical Apollonian circle packing. Tools from arithmetic groups, hyperbolic geometry, and dynamics are used to show that, on one hand, there is an infinite zoo of such objects, while on the other, there are essentially finitely many of these, in all dimensions. No familiarity with any of these topics will be assumed.

proof_stages.label: Apollonian arithmetic prototype
proof_stages.summary: The talk reviews Apollonian circle packings, the integrality of bends, the mod-24 local obstructions, and the local-global conjecture/density theorem that motivate the broader arithmetic problem.

proof_stages.label: Crystallographic and superintegral framework
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates the general problem by defining oriented sphere packings, crystallographic packings as limit sets of geometrically finite reflection groups, symmetry groups, supergroups, superpackings, and superintegrality, while stating the competing infinitude and finiteness phenomena.

proof_stages.label: Polyhedral cluster/co-cluster construction
proof_stages.summary: Using the Koebe–Andreev–Thurston midsphere theorem, a convex polyhedron is turned into a cluster and orthogonal co-cluster whose reflection orbit gives a crystallographic packing, reducing integrality questions to algebraic data that can be rigorously verified.

proof_stages.label: Infinitude from seed polyhedra and growth operations
proof_stages.summary: Integral and superintegral uniform seed polyhedra are identified, and gluing/doubling growth operations are introduced; the key observation is that the superpacking of a growth lies inside the seed superpacking, so superintegrality propagates to infinitely many conformally inequivalent packings.

proof_stages.label: Finiteness from arithmetic supergroups
proof_stages.summary: A structure theorem makes the supergroup of a crystallographic packing a finite-covolume hyperbolic reflection group, and superintegrality forces this lattice to be arithmetic; known finiteness and dimension-bounding theorems for arithmetic hyperbolic reflection groups then give finitely many commensurability classes.

proof_stages.label: Partial converse via Coxeter decompositions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker asks whether arithmetic reflection groups support superintegral crystallographic packings and proves this for nonuniform dimension-two Bianchi cases by finding cluster/co-cluster decompositions in Coxeter diagrams, while discussing the Eisenstein outlier, cocompact obstacles, and higher-dimensional evidence.

proof_stages.label: Integral but non-superintegral boundary cases
proof_stages.summary: The hexagonal pyramid/prism case is analyzed through reflections and Vinberg’s Gram-matrix cyclic-product criterion to show how an integral crystallographic packing can fail to be superintegral and have nonarithmetic, possibly quasi-arithmetic, supergroup behavior.

proof_stages.label: Local-global consequences and congruence obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Returning to bend local-global questions, the talk states a general asymptotic theorem for circle-packing orbits under a congruence stabilizer condition and notes that infinitely many packings satisfy it while infinitely many fail it.
